_comment: Discredited / scientifically-limited forensic-method reference for the DISCREDITED_FORENSIC_METHOD flag (README v2 Section 5.2; spec v3 Appendix A). Externalized from forensic.py so terms/tiers/citations are an editable, reviewable diff. Loaded by risk_engine.forensic. Order is preserved. tier: A formally invalidated/abandoned, B unvalidated per NAS 2009/PCAST 2016 but still used, C contested/evolving.

tier_meaning.B: unvalidated per NAS 2009 / PCAST 2016 but still in use
tier_meaning.C: contested or evolving in the literature

methods.source: PCAST 2016 found bitemark analysis not scientifically valid and unlikely to be salvageable; NAS 2009 Ch. 5 found no scientific basis for identifying a source from bitemarks.
methods.authority: Texas Forensic Science Commission moratorium recommendation (2016); NAS 2009

methods.source: NAS 2009 Ch. 5: no scientific basis for individualization by hair microscopy; FBI/DOJ 2015 review acknowledged flawed microscopic-hair testimony in the overwhelming majority of audited trials.
NAS 2004 'Forensic Analysis: Weighing Bullet Lead Evidence' rejected the supporting inferences; the FBI discontinued the technique in 2005.
methods.authority: FBI abandonment of the technique (2005); NAS 2004

methods.source: Pre-NFPA-921 fire 'pour pattern' / burn-indicator folklore (crazed glass, alligatoring, low burn) was invalidated as a basis for determining incendiary origin; NFPA 921 (1992 onward) is the accepted standard.
The diagnostic 'triad' as sole proof of abusive head trauma is contested in the biomechanical and medical literature; courts and reviews have flagged it as not scientifically settled.
PCAST 2016 found firearms/toolmark identification had not been established as foundationally valid and rested on a single appropriately-designed study with a non-trivial error rate.
PCAST 2016 found footwear/impression source-identification claims lacked established foundational validity for individualization.
PCAST 2016 found interpretation of complex DNA mixtures (multiple contributors, low template) foundationally valid only within narrow, validated bounds, and unreliable outside them.
NAS 2009 Ch. 5 found bloodstain-pattern analysis opinions more subjective than scientific and the uncertainties not well understood.
Scent-lineup / dog-scent identification has repeatedly figured in documented wrongful-conviction casework and lacks validated error rates (Innocence Project casework).
